title: Make systemd a proper compfuzor subsystem
description:
# Systemd Subsystem Refactoring

## Current State

Systemd handling is NOT a proper subsystem in the `doc/subsys.md` sense. It has no entry in `SUBSYSTEM` in `vars/common.yaml`, no `gen_systemd.tasks`, and no `subsys` lookup integration. Instead it is a bespoke pipeline spread across 5+ files that runs unconditionally.

## Key Files

| File | Purpose |
|------|---------|
| `vars/systemd.yaml` (413 lines) | Defaults, SYSTEMD_TYPE_CONFIG (14 unit types), phrase lists, scope gates, pkg-config-derived directory paths |
| `tasks/compfuzor/vars_systemd.tasks` (33 lines) | Runs `pkg-config systemd --variable=...` for 23 paths, loads defaults |
| `tasks/compfuzor/vars_systemd_unit.tasks` (187 lines) | Monolith: type detection, ETC_FILES generation, install script generation, SYSTEMD_ROOTS multi-unit, separate system/user scope |
| `tasks/systemd.thunk.tasks` (28 lines) | Legacy enable/restart during post-run phase |
| `tasks/systemd.unit.includes` (104 lines) | Older unit generation with .d directory handling, being replaced |
| `files/systemd.unit` (22 lines) | Section-based template rendering from merged dicts |
| `files/systemd/install-unit.sh` (22 lines) | Shared install helper: symlink + daemon-reload + enable |
| `files/systemd.service` | Old phrase-based template (legacy) |
| `skill/systemd.md` (244 lines) | User-facing documentation |

## Problems

### 1. Runs unconditionally
`vars_systemd_unit.tasks` is imported at `compfuzor.includes:24` with no `when:` guard. Every playbook gets systemd processing even if it defines no units. This violates the subsystem pattern where `gen_*.tasks` should be guarded by the trigger condition.

### 2. Not a subsystem
No entry in `SUBSYSTEM` dict, no `gen_systemd.tasks`, no `subsys` lookup. The systemd "subsystem" is split between vars processing and task includes rather than following the standard pattern described in `doc/subsys.md`.

### 3. 187-line monolith in vars_systemd_unit.tasks
This single task file handles: type detection (`_systemd_has_service`, `_systemd_has_socket`, etc.), ETC_FILES generation for single units, ETC_FILES generation from SYSTEMD_ROOTS, install-unit.sh BINS injection, per-type per-scope install script generation. All of this should be decomposed.

### 4. User service install requires hand-written scripts
Of ~36 playbooks with `install-user.sh`, nearly all that deal with systemd user services hand-roll: `mkdir -p ~/.config/systemd/user`, symlink, daemon-reload, enable, start. The auto-generated `install-service-user.sh` exists but:
- Only enables, does not start (no `--now`)
- Is not well-known to playbook authors
- Requires understanding SYSTEMD_INSTALL=user/both/separate to trigger

### 5. install-unit.sh does not start services
The generated install scripts only `enable` units, never `enable --now` or `start`. For user services especially, authors expect the service to start immediately.

### 6. No EnvironmentFile integration
The systemd unit template (`files/systemd.unit`) renders section dicts but has no awareness of the generated `env.export` file. Playbooks must manually add `EnvironmentFile=` or `Environment=` directives. The env file (generated from `files/_env` template) contains all the playbook ENV vars and is available at `$DIR/env.export`, but the systemd unit does not reference it.

### 7. Scope confusion for non-USERMODE playbooks
Many `.src.pb` playbooks need a user systemd service but are NOT user-mode (they install binaries system-wide, need system PKGS, etc.). The current system conflates USERMODE with systemd user scope. Getting a user service in a system-mode playbook requires setting SYSTEMD_SCOPE, SYSTEMD_INSTALL, and SYSTEMD_INSTALLS.WantedBy explicitly. There is no simple "just give me a user service" affordance.

### 8. Dual codepaths
`systemd.unit.includes` (old) and `vars_systemd_unit.tasks` (new) coexist. The old path handles .d directory config overlays. The thunk.tasks still runs for enable/restart. The migration from old to new is incomplete.

## The Three-Section Dict Pattern (works well)

The current pattern of three dicts mapping to [Unit], [Service], [Install] sections is good:
- `SYSTEMD_UNITS` -> [Unit]
- `SYSTEMD_SERVICES` -> [Service] (or type-specific section)
- `SYSTEMD_INSTALLS` -> [Install]

These merge on top of defaults from `vars/systemd.yaml`. The `files/systemd.unit` template iterates section names from `SYSTEMD_TYPE_CONFIG` and renders key=value pairs. This is clean and should be preserved.

## What a Proper Subsystem Would Look Like

Following `doc/subsys.md`:

1. Add `systemd` entry to `SUBSYSTEM` in `vars/common.yaml` with:
   - `requested`: triggered by SYSTEMD_SERVICE/SOCKET/MOUNT or SYSTEMD_SERVICES/SOCKETS/MOUNTS being defined
   - `contrib`: install-unit.sh helper and generated type-specific install scripts as BINS

2. Create `gen_systemd.tasks` — merge BINS (install scripts) and ETC_FILES (unit files), guarded by `lookup(subsys, id=systemd, get=active)`

3. Guard the import in `compfuzor.includes` with `when:` matching the trigger

4. Keep `vars_systemd.tasks` (pkg-config probe) running conditionally or making paths available on-demand

5. Migrate `systemd.thunk.tasks` behavior into the generated install scripts (enable + restart)

6. Add `install-user.sh` as a generated BINS entry when user scope is active, covering the common case of: create dir, symlink unit, daemon-reload, enable --now

## Migration Path

1. Add systemd to SUBSYSTEM with requested trigger
2. Create gen_systemd.tasks with the unit generation + install script logic
3. Guard compfuzor.includes import
4. Add install-user.sh generation for user scope
5. Add EnvironmentFile= awareness to unit template
6. Retire systemd.unit.includes and systemd.thunk.tasks
7. Migrate playbooks from hand-written install-user.sh to generated one
8. Consider making install-unit.sh do enable --now by default

title: LINKS should co-author install.sh instead of requiring manual symlinks
description:
Currently LINKS creates symlinks during the filesystem phase. We want LINKS entries to also be emitted into install.sh scripts so that deployment is a single script run. This would let vars_kernel.tasks (and other generative subsystems) add LINKS that get co-authored into a shared install.sh, avoiding the need for separate manual symlink creation steps.

The install.sh pattern: build.sh generates files, install.sh deploys them (symlinks into system dirs). LINKS should feed into install.sh automatically.

title: Add dagger CLI .src.pb
description:
Create a `dagger.src.pb` playbook for the Dagger CI/CD CLI tool (https://github.com/dagger/dagger).

Dagger is a programmable CI/CD engine that runs pipelines as code in containers. The CLI binary is built from Go source.

The `.src.pb` should follow the compfuzor pattern for Go-based source builds:
- Set TYPE to `dagger`, REPO to `https://github.com/dagger/dagger`
- Include `go: True` in TOOL_VERSIONS
- Build via `go build` or the project's Makefile
- Install the `dagger` binary to GLOBAL_BINS_DIR
- Import `tasks/compfuzor.includes`
close_reason: Already implemented. dagger.src.pb exists with GO: True, GO_TARGET: ./cmd/dagger, GO_BIN: dagger.

title: Emit systemd sysext and confext layers
description:
Add support for generating systemd system extension (sysext) and configuration extension (confext) layers.

These are OS-level extension mechanisms in systemd that allow stacking additional filesystem layers on top of the base system:
- sysext: System extensions for adding binaries, libraries, and other system files
- confext: Configuration extensions for adding drop-in configuration

Both use raw GPT disk images with specific partition types and can be merged at runtime via systemd-sysext/systemd-confext.

title: Configure scroll speed across all applications
description:
Configure appropriate scroll speed settings across all applications used in the desktop environment:
- niri (compositor)
- kitty (terminal)
- wezterm (terminal)
- ghostty (terminal)
- opencode

Each application may have different mechanisms for adjusting scroll speed. This epic tracks the individual configuration tasks.

title: HuggingFace GGUF JSON Variant Scraper
description: Create a robust Node.js script to scrape all JSON variants from HuggingFace GGUF model pages using lol-html for HTML parsing.
acceptance_criteria:
- Script successfully scrapes JSON variant files from HuggingFace GGUF pages
- Uses lol-html for efficient HTML parsing
- Handles various page structures gracefully
- Outputs to files/llama-cpp/ directory
- Includes error handling and logging
- Well-documented and maintainable code

title: Audit when-clauses for redundant downstream defaults
description:
Many tasks guard with `when: X is defined` or `when: X is truthy` but then use `X|default(...)` downstream in the same task block. When the `when` already guarantees the variable exists/is truthy, the `default` filter is dead code that adds noise and cognitive load.

Example from `vars_repo.tasks`:
```yaml
when: \"REPOS is defined\"
with_items: \"{{(REPOS|default({})).keys()}}\"
```
The `when` ensures REPOS exists, so `|default({})` can never fire.

Scope: scan all `.tasks` files for patterns where a `when` condition proves a variable is defined/truthy but `|default(...)` is still used on that same variable within the task body. Also look for `|bool` casts on values already guaranteed boolean by the when-clause, and similar redundant defensive patterns.

This is a cleanup/refactoring pass — no behavior changes, just removing dead defaults and casts where the `when` clause already constrains the value.
